data.title: Constellation Brands Resets Outlook As New CEO Refocuses On Core Beer
data.description:
• Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) withdrew its previously issued long term fiscal 2028 outlook, citing ongoing market uncertainty.
  • The company announced a leadership transition, with Nicholas Fink taking over the CEO role.
  • Management highlighted a sharper focus on core brands and stepped up marketing around events such as the World Cup.

Constellation Brands enters this shift with its shares at $166.15 and a mixed return profile, including a 17.7% gain year to date and a 24.2% decline over five years. That combination suggests investors have experienced meaningful volatility, which can make changes in leadership and long term guidance especially relevant to how you think about risk.

The new CEO and the pullback of fiscal 2028 targets put more attention on how NYSE:STZ executes on its core brand and marketing priorities from here. As the company leans into major events like the World Cup, investors may want to watch how these choices influence revenue mix, spending levels, and longer term margin potential.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Constellation Brands by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Constellation Brands.

Does the team leading Constellation Brands have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

The leadership change comes as Constellation Brands is recalibrating its visibility on the future. With Nicholas Fink stepping in as CEO just after the company reported full year revenue of US$9.1b and net income of US$1.7b, plus an 11.3% year on year sales decline in the latest quarter, investors are getting a new voice at a time when demand signals are mixed. Withdrawing the fiscal 2028 outlook and leaning into higher marketing around events like the World Cup shifts attention from long term targets to nearer term execution, especially in beer where consumption trends and Hispanic consumer spending have been under pressure. At the same time, the company has been returning cash through buybacks and a higher quarterly dividend, which suggests the board is comfortable with the balance sheet and cash generation for now. For you, the key question is whether this leadership reset and marketing push help stabilise volumes in core brands such as Modelo and Corona while keeping margins and capital returns on a steady footing.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Constellation Brands Narrative

  • The focus on core brands and World Cup related marketing lines up with the narrative that the beer portfolio, particularly Mexican imports, remains the main earnings driver and could support revenue once consumer sentiment improves.
  • Pulling the fiscal 2028 outlook and flagging subdued demand challenges assumptions that beer growth and margin expansion will progress smoothly, especially given earlier cuts to long term beer growth expectations.
  • The timing and impact of the CEO transition, along with weaker volumes among key Hispanic consumers, may not be fully reflected in narrative assumptions that emphasise cost savings and brewery expansion in Veracruz.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Constellation Brands to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ High debt remains a flagged risk, so heavier marketing spend and softer beer demand could put more pressure on cash flows if volumes do not stabilise.
  • ⚠️ The withdrawal of long term guidance and softer outlook for beer growth raise uncertainty about how quickly trends in alcohol consumption, including weaker spending by Hispanic consumers, may stabilise.
  • 🎁 Constellation Brands has returned to profitability with about US$1.7b in net income over the last year and continues to generate strong cash, supporting dividends and share repurchases.
  • 🎁 A sharper focus on core brands and upcoming World Cup activations could help defend or grow share in premium beer against competitors like Anheuser Busch InBev and Heineken if execution and consumer engagement land well.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how Nicholas Fink sets priorities around beer volumes, pricing, and marketing efficiency, especially into fiscal 2027. Quarterly updates on depletion trends, response to World Cup campaigns, and any commentary on Hispanic consumer behaviour will be important signals for how resilient the core beer portfolio really is. Investors should also watch whether margin trends in beer, wine, and spirits stay consistent with management commentary while the company continues buybacks and a higher dividend. Any fresh guidance that replaces the withdrawn fiscal 2028 outlook, or more detail on how cost savings and the new brewery are tracking, will help clarify whether this leadership transition tightens or loosens the company’s risk profile.

Approach 1: E.ON Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model looks at the value of a stock by projecting all future dividend payments and discounting them back to today. It is essentially asking what those dividend streams are worth in € right now.

For E.ON, the model uses a current dividend per share of €0.62, a return on equity of 11.10% and a payout ratio of about 77%. Future dividend growth in the model is set at 1.73%, capped from a higher starting figure, with an expected growth input of 2.55%. This combination assumes E.ON continues to pay out a large share of earnings while growing dividends at a modest pace.

Based on these inputs, the DDM arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €18.46 per share. Compared with the current share price of €19.59, that indicates the stock is about 6.1% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap and well within the sort of range income investors often tolerate for a large utility.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk typically support a higher P/E, while slower growth and higher risk usually point to a lower, more conservative range.

data.title: Eurobio Scientific (ENXTPA:ALERS) Margin Improvement To 2.9% Tests Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme (ENXTPA:ALERS) has reported FY 2025 first half revenue of €80.8 million and basic EPS of €0.23, setting the tone for a year where margins and earnings quality are firmly in focus for investors. The company has seen revenue move from €73.3 million in the first half of 2024 to €81.5 million in the second half of 2024 and then to €80.8 million in the latest period, while basic EPS shifted from €0.40 to effectively flat and now €0.23, against a backdrop of a 2.9% net profit margin over the last 12 months that frames a tighter, margin-driven story rather than a pure topline one.

2.9% net margin points to tight profitability

  • Over the last 12 months, Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme converted €169 million of revenue into €4.9 million of net income, which works out to a 2.9% net margin compared with 2.6% a year earlier.
  • What challenges the bearish worry about a collapsing business is that, despite a 61% per year earnings decline over five years, the most recent year showed 22.6% earnings growth versus that five year average, while recent half year revenues held in a tight band between €73.3 million and €81.5 million.
    • Skeptics highlight the long run earnings decline, yet the trailing 12 month margin and €4.9 million net income sit above the older five year average earnings level.
    • Bears also focus on earnings volatility, but the last three reported halves show revenue in the €73 million to €81.5 million range, which gives some underpinning to that recent margin improvement.

Multi year earnings slide meets richer 47.9x P/E

  • The shares trade on a 47.9x trailing P/E, well above both the European Biotechs industry at 15.2x and a peer group at 14.7x, while reported earnings have declined by 61% per year over the past five years.
  • Bears argue that such a rich multiple is hard to square with the long run earnings slide, and the numbers give that concern some weight because the 47.9x P/E is over 3x the sector and peer averages even after that 61% annual decline in earnings.
    • Critics highlight that paying more than 3x the industry P/E exposes holders if earnings were to revert closer to the weaker multi year trend.
    • What complicates the bearish call is that the last 12 months delivered 22.6% earnings growth versus the five year average, so the P/E is being set against a year that is better than the longer history.

DCF fair value of €52.98 vs €23.30 price

  • A DCF fair value of €52.98 compared with a share price of €23.30 suggests the stock trades about 56% below that cash flow based estimate, even as the trailing 12 month net margin sits at 2.9%.
  • Supporters of a more bullish stance point out that this DCF gap heavily supports the idea of mispricing, yet the high 47.9x P/E and the 61% per year five year earnings decline mean investors still need to reconcile a model implying upside with market metrics that price in a lot of past volatility.

data.title: Al Ramz Real Estate's (TADAWUL:4327) Earnings Are Weaker Than They Seem
data.description:
Despite announcing strong earnings, Al Ramz Real Estate Company's (TADAWUL:4327) stock was sluggish. We think that the market might be paying attention to some underlying factors that they find to be concerning.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against Al Ramz Real Estate's Earnings

As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

For the year to December 2025, Al Ramz Real Estate had an accrual ratio of 0.72. As a general rule, that bodes poorly for future profitability. And indeed, during the period the company didn't produce any free cash flow whatsoever. Over the last year it actually had negative free cash flow of ر.س760m, in contrast to the aforementioned profit of ر.س281.0m. We also note that Al Ramz Real Estate's free cash flow was actually negative last year as well, so we could understand if shareholders were bothered by its outflow of ر.س760m.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Al Ramz Real Estate.

Our Take On Al Ramz Real Estate's Profit Performance

As we discussed above, we think Al Ramz Real Estate's earnings were not supported by free cash flow, which might concern some investors. As a result, we think it may well be the case that Al Ramz Real Estate's underlying earnings power is lower than its statutory profit. But the good news is that its EPS growth over the last three years has been very impressive. The goal of this article has been to assess how well we can rely on the statutory earnings to reflect the company's potential, but there is plenty more to consider. If you want to do dive deeper into Al Ramz Real Estate, you'd also look into what risks it is currently facing. Approach 1: Crane Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to today using a required return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Crane, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $347.6 million. Analysts provide forecasts out to 2030, and those figures are then extended further by Simply Wall St. Within this, projected free cash flow for 2030 is $620.2 million, with interim years such as 2026 and 2027 at $341.2 million and $440.8 million respectively, all in dollars. These projected cash flows are then discounted. For example, the 2026 and 2030 discounted values are $314.3 million and $411.3 million.

Putting this together, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $189.65 per share. Compared to the recent share price of $188.46, the implied discount is around 0.6%, which points to Crane trading very close to this cash flow based estimate.
